title: Parmigiana di melanzane
subtitle: Baked layers of aubergine, tomato sauce, mozzarella, and Parmesan
excerpt: Slices of aubergine salted, dried, and fried until soft and golden, then layered with a simple tomato sauce, torn mozzarella, fresh basil, and grated Parmesan, and baked until bubbling and set. A dish that improves significantly when made a day ahead and reheated.
sourceNotes: Parmigiana di melanzane is claimed by both Sicily and the Campania region. The dish appears in Italian cookery literature from the nineteenth century. The combination of fried aubergine, tomato, and cheese is described in various southern Italian household recipe collections from the 1880s onward. No single public-domain text; multiple cross-referenced Italian regional sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before the final bake. Defrost overnight and bake from cold, adding 15 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the whole dish a day ahead, cover, and refrigerate. Reheat at 180°C for 25 minutes covered, then 10 minutes uncovered.

content.text: Parmigiana di melanzane is not a quick dish. Salting the aubergine slices draws out moisture and prevents them absorbing too much oil when fried. Allow at least an hour for this. Once fried, the slices are layered with tomato sauce, mozzarella, and Parmesan, then baked until the layers have melted together. The dish needs to rest for at least 15 minutes before cutting — eaten immediately from the oven it will collapse on the plate.

Making this a day ahead is the better approach. The flavours intensify overnight and the layers hold together when reheated. The tomato sauce should be cooked down to a thick, concentrated consistency before the layering starts. A watery sauce produces a soggy result.

Lay the {{aubergine}} aubergine slices in a colander in layers, salting each layer generously with the salting salt. Set the colander over a bowl or in the sink. Leave for 1 hour. Rinse the slices well under cold water and pat thoroughly dry with kitchen paper — the drier they are, the less oil they absorb when frying.

Heat the sauce {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute without colouring. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and a generous pinch of salt. Add half the {{basil}} of basil leaves. Simmer for 25 to 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is thick and concentrated. Taste and adjust the seasoning.

Heat the frying {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry the aubergine slices in batches for 2 to 3 minutes per side until golden and softened. Drain on kitchen paper. Add more oil to the pan between batches as needed.

Heat the oven to 200°C (fan 180°C). Spread a thin layer of tomato sauce over the base of a rectangular baking tin. Add a layer of fried aubergine. Top with more sauce, some of the {{mozzarella}} torn mozzarella, a few of the remaining basil leaves, and a scatter of {{parmesan}} of Parmesan. Repeat the layers until all the ingredients are used, finishing with a layer of sauce and a generous covering of Parmesan. Bake for 30 minutes until golden and bubbling. Rest for 15 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Not rested, or layers too wet
items.fix: Rest for at least 15 minutes; ensure the tomato sauce is thick before layering and the aubergine is well dried

items.symptom: Aubergine has absorbed too much oil and is greasy
items.cause: Slices not dried after salting, or oil too cool when frying
items.fix: Pat the slices completely dry before frying; fry in batches in hot oil — cool oil produces oily results

items.symptom: Mozzarella has produced a pool of water in the layers
items.fix: Drain torn mozzarella on kitchen paper for 10 minutes before using; buffalo mozzarella releases more water than cow mozzarella

content.text: Sicily and Campania both claim parmigiana di melanzane. The name refers to the layering method, not to Parmesan or to Parma — the word parmiciana in Sicilian dialect describes a shutter arrangement of overlapping slats, which is how the aubergine slices are layered. It is primarily a summer dish, made when aubergines are at their peak, and eaten at room temperature as often as it is served hot.
